name: Security Misconfiguration
what_it_means: Unsafe defaults, incomplete hardening, forgotten debug settings, or misconfigured security controls leave the API exposed — including open CORS policies, verbose error messages that leak internals, missing security headers, unused HTTP methods, hardcoded secrets, and improperly secured cloud or infrastructure resources.
animated_example: Installing a new high-security front door but leaving the back windows open, the alarm disabled, and a spare key under the mat.
quick_checks: CORS policy restricted to an explicit origin allowlist — wildcard (*) never used in production for credentialed requests
All HTTP methods not explicitly required disabled (TRACE always disabled; OPTIONS restricted)
Error responses return generic messages — no stack traces, SQL errors, file paths, or framework versions
Full security response header set applied (HSTS, CSP, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, Referrer-Policy)
All secrets managed in a dedicated vault — never in source code, container images, or environment files
Configuration-as-code with security linting gates in CI/CD pipeline

payload_types.description: API configured with Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * (or a reflected origin) combined with Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true allows malicious web pages to make authenticated cross-origin API requests using the victim's session and read the responses.
examples: Origin: https://attacker.com sent to the API — response includes Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * and Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true, allowing cross-origin authenticated reads
API reflects any Origin header value back in Access-Control-Allow-Origin — attacker's page at https://evil.com receives * CORS permission, enabling full API access with victim's cookies
JavaScript on attacker-controlled page uses fetch() with credentials:include to call the victim API and exfiltrate response data via the permissive CORS policy
Null origin exploitation: ACAO: null combined with sandboxed iframe allows cross-origin reads without a real domain
Subdomain takeover combined with permissive CORS allowing all subdomains — attacker takes over unused subdomain and leverages CORS to read main API responses
mitigation: Define an explicit allowlist of trusted origins in the CORS configuration — never use wildcard (*) for endpoints that return authenticated or sensitive data.
Never combine Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true with a wildcard or reflected-origin CORS policy — credentials must only be allowed for explicitly listed, trusted origins.
Reject null as a valid CORS origin — null origin is sent by sandboxed iframes and file:// contexts that should never have cross-origin API access.
Validate the Origin header against the allowlist on the server side, not in client-side JavaScript — return 403 for untrusted origins rather than including them in the response header.
Regularly audit CORS configuration across all API endpoints using automated scanning — verify no endpoint inadvertently reflects arbitrary origins or uses wildcard for credentialed requests.

payload_types.type: Verbose Error and Stack Trace Leakage
payload_types.description: API returns detailed error messages, exception stack traces, SQL query text, file system paths, framework version strings, or internal service names to API consumers — providing attackers with reconnaissance information to plan targeted attacks.
examples: POST /api/login with malformed JSON returns a full Java stack trace including Spring Boot version, internal class paths, and database driver details
GET /api/users?id=abc causes a SQL error returned verbatim: 'ERROR: invalid input syntax for integer, query: SELECT * FROM users WHERE id=abc'
Internal service error leaks the full exception: 'Connection refused: redis://internal-cache:6379' — revealing internal hostname and port
404 response includes the full filesystem path: 'File not found: /opt/app/src/api/routes/users.js:47'
Unhandled exception returns: 'Django version 3.1.5 at /api/endpoint — you have DEBUG=True set' — exposing framework version and debug mode status
mitigation: Configure production environments to return only generic error messages (e.g., 'An error occurred. Please try again.') — map all internal exceptions to safe, user-facing error codes.
Implement a global exception handler that catches all unhandled errors, logs the full detail server-side, and returns only a sanitized error response to the client.
Disable debug mode in all production deployments — use environment-specific configuration profiles to ensure debug flags cannot be accidentally enabled in production.
Suppress framework version strings, server banners, and X-Powered-By headers in all production API responses.
Test all error paths in automated security tests: trigger validation errors, authentication errors, database errors, and 404s — inspect every response for internal information leakage.

payload_types.description: Framework diagnostic endpoints, admin consoles, health check endpoints, or development utilities remain accessible in production — often without authentication — leaking internal state or providing unauthorized administrative access.
examples: GET /actuator/env returns all Spring Boot environment variables including database credentials and API keys
GET /api/swagger.json in production exposes the complete API schema including internal and undocumented endpoints
GET /.well-known/security.txt returns but /admin/phpinfo.php is also accessible, leaking PHP version, loaded modules, and server configuration
GET /debug/vars (Go expvar endpoint) returns internal application metrics including memory stats and request counters
POST /api/graphql with introspection query returns the full schema including internal mutation types and admin fields not intended for external callers
mitigation: Block all framework diagnostic and actuator endpoints at the network layer in production — restrict /actuator, /metrics, /debug, /health/details to internal monitoring system IPs only.
Disable or password-protect Swagger/OpenAPI documentation endpoints in production — or serve only the public subset of the API schema without internal endpoints.
Disable GraphQL introspection in production environments — if introspection is required for tooling, gate it behind admin authentication.
Implement automated endpoint discovery scans post-deployment to detect debug or admin paths that appear in production builds.
Use deployment pipeline checks to assert that known debug endpoint paths return 404 or 403 in production before every release goes live.

payload_types.description: API responses lack critical security response headers — or headers are present but misconfigured — leaving clients vulnerable to clickjacking, MIME-type sniffing, cross-site scripting, and information disclosure through browser security feature bypass.
examples: Missing Strict-Transport-Security header — browser does not enforce HTTPS, allowing SSL stripping attacks on subsequent requests
Content-Security-Policy: * allows loading resources from any origin, negating XSS protection for API-driven SPAs
Missing X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff — browser MIME-sniffs responses, potentially executing JSON as JavaScript
X-Frame-Options absent — API responses embedded in iframes, enabling clickjacking attacks against API-driven UI components
Cache-Control: no headers set on responses containing sensitive data — private API responses cached by shared proxies or browser cache
mitigation: Apply the full mandatory security header set to all API responses: Strict-Transport-Security (max-age≥31536000, includeSubDomains, preload), X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff, X-Frame-Options: DENY, Referrer-Policy: no-referrer.
Define a strict Content-Security-Policy for API-driven web applications — restrict script-src, connect-src, and frame-ancestors to explicitly listed trusted sources.
Set Cache-Control: no-store, no-cache on all API responses containing authentication tokens, personal data, or sensitive business information.
Automate security header validation in your post-deployment pipeline using tools like SecurityHeaders.io or OWASP ZAP — fail the deployment if required headers are missing or misconfigured.
Apply HSTS preload submission for all production API domains to protect against SSL stripping even on first visit.

payload_types.description: API keys, database credentials, signing secrets, or admin passwords are hardcoded in source code, container images, environment files, or deployment scripts — or default credentials from frameworks and tools have not been changed before deployment.
examples: JWT signing secret hardcoded as 'secret' or 'changeme' in application source code committed to a public repository
Database connection string with credentials committed to a .env file included in a public Docker image layer
Admin console accessible with default credentials admin/admin or admin/password — never changed after deployment
API key for a payment provider hardcoded in a mobile app's strings.xml file, extractable by decompiling the APK
AWS access key and secret hardcoded in a utility script pushed to a public GitHub repository — discovered and abused within minutes via automated credential scanning
mitigation: Store all secrets (API keys, database credentials, signing keys, certificates) in a dedicated secrets management system (HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault, GCP Secret Manager) — never in source code or container images.
Implement pre-commit secret scanning hooks (detect-secrets, git-secrets, truffleHog) to block accidental credential commits before they reach the repository.
Enforce mandatory credential rotation on a defined schedule with automated expiry alerts — treat non-rotating credentials as a security risk.
Change all default credentials (admin consoles, database root users, framework management interfaces) before deployment and audit for default credentials as part of deployment checklists.

Continuously scan public repositories, container registries, and artifact stores for leaked secrets using automated tools — establish an immediate rotation and revocation procedure when leaks are detected.

how_to_test: Step 1: Send preflight CORS requests with Origin: https://attacker.com and verify the response does not reflect untrusted origins or use wildcard with credentials
Step 2: Trigger validation errors, authentication failures, and 404s — inspect all error responses for stack traces, SQL text, file paths, or framework version strings
Step 3: Probe known framework diagnostic paths (/actuator, /debug, /metrics, /.env, /swagger.json, /graphql with introspection) and verify all return 404 or 403 in production
Step 4: Inspect all API response headers against the required security header set — flag any missing headers (HSTS, CSP, X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, Referrer-Policy)
Step 5: Attempt access with default credentials (admin/admin, admin/password, root/root) on all management interfaces — verify all defaults have been changed
Step 6: Scan source code repositories, container images, and CI/CD pipelines for hardcoded secrets using automated detection tools
Step 7: Send OPTIONS and TRACE requests to every endpoint — verify TRACE is disabled and OPTIONS does not advertise unintended or dangerous methods
expected_outcomes: CORS responses never include untrusted origins or combine wildcard with credentials
Error responses contain only generic messages — no internal paths, stack traces, SQL text, or framework versions
All diagnostic and debug endpoints return 403 or 404 in production
All required security headers present on every API response with correct values
No default or hardcoded credentials accessible — all secrets managed in a vault with rotation enforced
minimal_example: Wildcard CORS + verbose SQL error → critical misconfiguration; fix: strict origin allowlist + generic error handler + full security headers applied post-deploy
mitigation: Lock down CORS to an explicit allowlist of trusted origins with defined methods and headers — never use wildcard (*) in production or combine with Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true

Apply the full security response header set (HSTS with preload, CSP, X-Frame-Options: DENY, X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff, Referrer-Policy) and validate automatically post-deploy with a scanning tool
Manage all secrets in a dedicated vault (HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault) — never commit secrets to source code or container images; implement pre-commit secret scanning hooks

Implement configuration-as-code with security linting gates in CI/CD to catch insecure defaults; return only generic error messages in production; block all debug and actuator endpoints at the network layer
